findings.title: SSRF via untrusted sessionWebhook URL in DingTalk sendMessage
findings.description: The defaultWebhookSender function issues an HTTP POST to an arbitrary URL. The sessionWebhook URL originates from incoming DingTalk bot messages (line 186-190), which is external untrusted input. An attacker who controls message content could craft a sessionWebhook pointing to internal services (e.g., http://169.254.169.254 for cloud metadata, or internal APIs), causing Server-Side Request Forgery. The getSessionWebhook method (line 162) makes this URL accessible to callers, and sendMessage (line 137) passes the target directly to defaultWebhookSender without validation.
findings.suggestion: Validate the webhook URL before making the request: (1) Parse with new URL(webhookUrl), (2) Allowlist the hostname to oapi.dingtalk.com or a configurable set of allowed domains, (3) Reject private/internal IP ranges. Example: create a validateWebhookUrl(url: string, allowedHosts: string[]) function that throws on invalid URLs.

findings.title: Package has zero consumers -- all new channels are orphan code
findings.code_snippet:
export { DingTalkChannel } from './dingtalk/index.js'
export { EmailChannel } from './email/index.js'
export { FeishuChannel } from './feishu/index.js'
findings.description: No package.json in the monorepo depends on @proj-airi/channels-extra. grep for '@proj-airi/channels-extra' across all .ts files returns zero hits. The three new channel implementations (EmailChannel, FeishuChannel, DingTalkChannel) are exported but not imported or wired to any entry point (HTTP handler, bot service, Electron main process, CLI). This is dead-on-arrival code per the integration rules. The existing SlackChannel and WhatsAppChannel have the same problem, suggesting the package itself has never been integrated.
findings.suggestion: Wire at least one channel to a live entry point to prove end-to-end connectivity. For example, register EmailChannel in an Electron main service or a server-runtime plugin, and add the @proj-airi/channels-extra dependency to that consumer's package.json. Without this, the vertical slice is incomplete.

findings.title: DingTalk sessionWebhooks Map grows without bound
findings.description: The sessionWebhooks Map accumulates entries for every unique conversationId received. Entries are only removed on individual expiry check (getSessionWebhook) or disconnect. There is no size cap, no periodic eviction, and no LRU policy. In a high-traffic DingTalk bot, this Map will grow indefinitely as new conversations arrive, causing memory pressure. The expiry check in getSessionWebhook (line 166) only fires on read -- expired entries for conversations never queried again remain in memory forever.
findings.suggestion: Add a maximum size cap (e.g., 10000 entries) with LRU eviction, or implement periodic cleanup of expired entries. Consider using a TTL-aware Map implementation. At minimum, add a sweepExpired() method called periodically or on every N insertions.

findings.title: No email address validation on sendMessage target
findings.description: The sendMessage method accepts an arbitrary string as the target email address and passes it directly to SMTP sendMail without any validation. This could lead to SMTP header injection if the target contains newlines or special characters (e.g., 'victim@example.com\r\nBcc: attacker@evil.com'). Additionally, malformed addresses could cause SMTP errors that are difficult to diagnose.
findings.suggestion: Validate the target email address before passing it to sendMail. Use a validation library (e.g., the 'validator' package isEmail function, or a Valibot email schema) to verify the format. At minimum, reject addresses containing \r or \n characters.

findings.title: Feishu EventDispatcher depends on SDK internal _handlers Map
findings.description: The FeishuEventDispatcher interface models the Feishu SDK's internal implementation detail (_handlers is a private Map). The underscore prefix convention indicates this is not a public API. If the Feishu SDK changes its internal structure (renames _handlers, switches to a different data structure, or makes it truly private), this code will silently break at runtime. The createEventDispatcher method (line 158) constructs this internal structure directly, bypassing any official SDK registration API.
findings.suggestion: Use the official Feishu SDK EventDispatcher constructor and its public registration API instead of constructing the internal _handlers Map directly. If the SDK requires this pattern, add a comment documenting the specific SDK version this depends on and a link to the SDK source, so the coupling is tracked.

findings.title: Hardcoded email subject line 'Message from Anima'
findings.description: The email subject is hardcoded to 'Message from Anima'. This should be configurable -- different deployments or contexts may need different subject lines. The MessageContent type only has a 'text' field, so there is no way for callers to specify a subject.
findings.suggestion: Either add an optional 'subject' field to MessageContent, or add a 'defaultSubject' field to EmailConfig, or accept a subject parameter in sendMessage. At minimum, make this a config value rather than a hardcoded string.

findings.title: Unsafe 'as' cast on IMAP 'exists' event data
findings.code_snippet:
this.imapClient.on('exists', (data: unknown) => {
      const existsData = data as { path: string, count: number, prevCount: number }
findings.description: The IMAP 'exists' event handler casts unknown data to a specific shape without runtime validation. If the imapflow library changes the event payload shape, or if a different IMAP client is injected, this will silently produce incorrect behavior (e.g., NaN from undefined arithmetic on line 169). The cast bypasses TypeScript's type safety at a package boundary.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime type guard (similar to isValidSlackMessage in slack/index.ts) that validates the data shape before accessing its properties. Return early with a warning log if the shape doesn't match.

findings.title: Naive email body extraction ignores MIME encoding
findings.description: The extractTextFromSource method splits on the first \r\n\r\n boundary and returns everything after as text. This ignores: (1) MIME multipart messages (will return raw MIME boundary markers), (2) Content-Transfer-Encoding (base64, quoted-printable will be returned encoded), (3) charset encoding (assumes UTF-8). Real-world emails are almost always MIME-encoded, so this will produce garbled text for most incoming messages.
findings.suggestion: Use the 'mailparser' library (simpleParser or parseMessage) to properly extract text content from email source. It handles MIME, encodings, and multipart messages correctly. This is a classic NIH violation -- email parsing is notoriously complex and has mature library solutions.

findings.title: Three channels added with no vertical slice integration
findings.code_snippet:
export { DingTalkChannel } from './dingtalk/index.js'
export { EmailChannel } from './email/index.js'
export { FeishuChannel } from './feishu/index.js'
findings.description: This change adds three horizontal channel implementations simultaneously without delivering any end-to-end vertical slice. None of the three channels is wired to a use case, registered in a service, or connected to any UI configuration. This is the 'horizontal layer' anti-pattern: build all implementations first, integrate later. The integration rules require each task to deliver at least one thin working path from entry point to persistence.
findings.suggestion: Pick one channel (e.g., EmailChannel as it has the simplest infrastructure requirements) and wire it end-to-end: register it in a service, add config UI, and prove a message can flow through. Then add the remaining channels incrementally.

findings.title: Error messages include user-supplied target in plaintext
findings.description: Error messages in sendMessage across all three channels include the target parameter verbatim. For Email this is an email address, for DingTalk this is a webhook URL. If these errors propagate to logs or user-facing responses, they could expose PII (email addresses) or internal URLs. This pattern is consistent across all three channels (email line 140, feishu line 131, dingtalk line 140).
findings.suggestion: Truncate or mask the target in error messages. For email: mask the address (e.g., 't***@example.com'). For webhook URLs: log only the domain, not the full URL with query parameters.

positive_observations: Consistent DI pattern across all three channels: constructor deps injection matches existing SlackChannel/WhatsAppChannel convention exactly
All three channels use @guiiai/logg with proper namespaced logger instances (channels-extra:email, channels-extra:feishu, channels-extra:dingtalk)
Test files include proper Test Double rationale comments explaining why external SDK mocking is necessary
Error handling in connect/disconnect follows the established pattern: set error status, throw with cause chain
Message handler dispatch has both sync try/catch and async .catch() to prevent unhandled rejections
DingTalk channel properly handles session webhook expiry with TTL-based validation in getSessionWebhook
Feishu channel correctly filters out bot self-messages to prevent echo loops
Clean separation of interface types (ImapClient, SmtpTransport, FeishuClient, StreamClient) from implementation -- enables proper dependency injection
